    82‚Üítheorem mod_100_of_comesFromPrimeQuadruple {n : ‚Ñï} (h : ComesFromPrimeQuadruple n) :
    83‚Üí    n % 100 = 9 := by
    84‚Üí  sorry
    85‚Üí
    86‚Üíend OeisA56777
    87‚Üí

    16‚Üíimport FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    17‚Üí
    18‚Üí/-!
    19‚Üí# Conjectures associated with A063880
    20‚Üí
    21‚ÜíA063880 lists numbers $n$ such that $\sigma(n) = 2 \cdot \text{usigma}(n)$, where $\sigma(n)$ is the
    22‚Üísum of all divisors and $\text{usigma}(n)$ is the sum of unitary divisors.
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíEquivalently, these are numbers whose unitary and non-unitary divisors have equal sum.
    25‚Üí
    26‚ÜíThe conjectures state that all members satisfy $n \equiv 108 \pmod{216}$, and that all
    27‚Üíprimitive terms (those whose proper divisors aren't in the sequence) are powerful numbers,
    28‚Üíwith $108$ being the only primitive term.
    29‚Üí
    30‚Üí*References:* [A063880](https://oeis.org/A063880)
    31‚Üí-/
    32‚Üí
    33‚Üíopen scoped ArithmeticFunction.sigma
    34‚Üí
    35‚Üínamespace OeisA63880
    36‚Üí
    37‚Üí/-- The set of unitary divisors of $n$: divisors $d$ such that $\gcd(d, n/d) = 1$. -/
    38‚Üídef unitaryDivisors (n : ‚Ñï) : Finset ‚Ñï :=
    39‚Üí  {d ‚àà n.divisors | d.Coprime (n / d)}
    40‚Üí
    41‚Üí/-- The sum of unitary divisors of $n$, denoted $\text{usigma}(n)$. -/
    42‚Üídef usigma (n : ‚Ñï) : ‚Ñï :=
    43‚Üí  ‚àë d ‚àà unitaryDivisors n, d
    44‚Üí
    45‚Üí/-- A number $n$ is in the sequence A063880 if $\sigma(n) = 2 \cdot \text{usigma}(n)$. -/
    46‚Üídef a (n : ‚Ñï) : Prop :=
    47‚Üí  0 < n ‚àß œÉ 1 n = 2 * usigma n
    48‚Üí
    49‚Üí/-- The set of numbers in the sequence A063880. -/
    50‚Üídef A : Set ‚Ñï := {n | a n}
    51‚Üí
    52‚Üí/-- A term $n$ is primitive if no proper divisor of $n$ is in the sequence. -/
    53‚Üíabbrev isPrimitiveTerm (n : ‚Ñï) : Prop := A.IsPrimitive n
    54‚Üí
    55‚Üí/-- $108$ is in the sequence A063880. -/
    56‚Üí@[category test, AMS 11]
    57‚Üítheorem a_108 : a 108 := by
    58‚Üí  refine ‚ü®by norm_num, ?_‚ü©
    59‚Üí  decide
    60‚Üí
    61‚Üí/-- $540$ is in the sequence A063880. -/
    62‚Üí@[category test, AMS 11]
    63‚Üítheorem a_540 : a 540 := by
    64‚Üí  refine ‚ü®by norm_num, ?_‚ü©
    65‚Üí  decide
    66‚Üí
    67‚Üí/-- $756$ is in the sequence A063880. -/
    68‚Üí@[category test, AMS 11]
    69‚Üítheorem a_756 : a 756 := by
    70‚Üí  refine ‚ü®by norm_num, ?_‚ü©
    71‚Üí  decide
    72‚Üí
    73‚Üí/-- $108$ is a primitive term. -/
    74‚Üí@[category test, AMS 11]
    75‚Üítheorem isPrimitiveTerm_108 : isPrimitiveTerm 108 := by
    76‚Üí  rw [isPrimitiveTerm, Set.isPrimitive_iff]
    77‚Üí  refine ‚ü®a_108, ?_‚ü©
    78‚Üí  intro d hd
    79‚Üí  have ‚ü®hdvd, hlt‚ü© := Nat.mem_properDivisors.mp hd
    80‚Üí  interval_cases d <;> simp_all [A, a] <;> decide
    81‚Üí
    82‚Üí/-- All members of the sequence satisfy $n \equiv 108 \pmod{216}$. -/
    83‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 11]
    84‚Üítheorem mod_216_of_a {n : ‚Ñï} (h : a n) : n % 216 = 108 := by
    85‚Üí  sorry
    86‚Üí
    87‚Üí/-- All primitive terms are powerful numbers. -/
    88‚Üí@[category undergraduate, AMS 11]
    89‚Üítheorem powerful_of_isPrimitiveTerm {n : ‚Ñï} (h : isPrimitiveTerm n) : n.Powerful := by
    90‚Üí  sorry
    91‚Üí
    92‚Üí/-- $108$ is the only primitive term. -/
    93‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 11]
    94‚Üítheorem unique_primitive_108 {n : ‚Ñï} (h : isPrimitiveTerm n) : n = 108 := by
    95‚Üí  sorry
    96‚Üí
    97‚Üí/-- If $m$ is a primitive term and $s$ is squarefree with $\gcd(m, s) = 1$, then $m \cdot s$
    98‚Üíis in the sequence. -/
    99‚Üí@[category undergraduate, AMS 11]
   100‚Üítheorem a_of_primitive_mul_squarefree (m s : ‚Ñï) (hm : isPrimitiveTerm m)
   101‚Üí    (hs : Squarefree s) (hcoprime : m.Coprime s) : a (m * s) := by
   102‚Üí  sorry
   103‚Üí
   104‚Üí/-- Non-primitive terms have the form $m \cdot s$ where $m$ is primitive and $s$ is
   105‚Üísquarefree with $\gcd(m, s) = 1$. -/
   106‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11]
   107‚Üítheorem exists_primitive_of_a {n : ‚Ñï} (h : a n) :
   108‚Üí    ‚àÉ m s, isPrimitiveTerm m ‚àß Squarefree s ‚àß m.Coprime s ‚àß n = m * s := by
   109‚Üí  sorry
   110‚Üí
   111‚Üíend OeisA63880
   112‚Üí

    20‚Üí/-!
    21‚Üí# Erd≈ës Problem 306
    22‚Üí
    23‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/306](https://www.erdosproblems.com/306)
    24‚Üí-/
    25‚Üí
    26‚Üíopen ArithmeticFunction
    27‚Üíopen scoped omega Omega

    29‚Üínamespace Erdos306
    30‚Üí
    31‚Üí/--
    32‚ÜíLet $\frac a b\in \mathbb{Q}_{>0}$ with $b$ squarefree. Are there integers $1 < n_1 < \dots < n_k$,
    33‚Üíeach the product of two distinct primes, such that $\frac{a}{b}=\frac{1}{n_1}+\cdots+\frac{1}{n_k}$?
    34‚Üí-/
    35‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 11]
    36‚Üítheorem erdos_306 : answer(sorry) ‚Üî ‚àÄ (q : ‚Ñö), 0 < q ‚Üí Squarefree q.den ‚Üí
    37‚Üí    ‚àÉ k : ‚Ñï, ‚àÉ (n : Fin (k + 1) ‚Üí ‚Ñï), n 0 = 1 ‚àß StrictMono n ‚àß
    38‚Üí    (‚àÄ i ‚àà Finset.Icc 1 (Fin.last k), œâ (n i) = 2 ‚àß Œ© (n i) = 2) ‚àß
    39‚Üí    q = ‚àë i ‚àà Finset.Icc 1 (Fin.last k), (1 : ‚Ñö) / (n i) := by
    40‚Üí  sorry
    41‚Üí
    42‚Üí/--
    43‚ÜíEvery positive integer can be expressed as an Egyptian fraction where each denominator is the
    44‚Üíproduct of three distinct primes.
    45‚Üí-/
    46‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    47‚Üítheorem erdos_306.variants.integer_three_primes (m : ‚Ñï) (h : 0 < m) :
    48‚Üí    ‚àÉ k > (0 : ‚Ñï), ‚àÉ (n : Fin (k + 1) ‚Üí ‚Ñï), n 0 = 1 ‚àß
    49‚Üí    ‚àÄ i, (hik : i < k) ‚Üí n ‚ü®i, by omega‚ü© < n ‚ü®(i + 1), by omega‚ü© ‚àß
    50‚Üí    (‚àÄ i ‚àà Finset.Icc 1 (Fin.last k), œâ (n i) = 3 ‚àß Œ© (n i) = 3) ‚àß
    51‚Üí    m = ‚àë i ‚àà Finset.Icc 1 (Fin.last k), (1 : ‚Ñö) / (n i) := by
    52‚Üí  sorry
    53‚Üí
    54‚Üíend Erdos306

    17‚Üíimport Mathlib.Data.Nat.Squarefree
    18‚Üí
    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# Primitive elements of a set with respect to divisibility
    21‚Üí
    22‚ÜíAn element `n` is *primitive* in a set `S ‚äÜ ‚Ñï` if `n ‚àà S` and no proper divisor of `n` is in `S`.
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíThis concept appears naturally when studying sequences closed under "divisor inheritance" -
    25‚Üíif every member `n` of a sequence has the property that `m * n` is also in the sequence
    26‚Üífor coprime `m`, then the primitive elements generate the entire sequence.
    27‚Üí
    28‚Üí## Main definitions
    29‚Üí
    30‚Üí* `Set.IsPrimitive`: A natural number `n` is primitive in `S` if `n ‚àà S` and
    31‚Üí  `n.properDivisors` is disjoint from `S`.
    32‚Üí* `Set.primitives`: The set of primitive elements of `S`.
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üí## Main results
    35‚Üí
    36‚Üí* `Set.IsPrimitive.mem`: A primitive element is in the set.
    37‚Üí* `Set.IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_properDivisor`: Proper divisors of a primitive element are not
    38‚Üí  in the set.
    39‚Üí* `Set.one_isPrimitive_iff`: `1` is primitive in `S` iff `1 ‚àà S`.
    40‚Üí* `Set.prime_isPrimitive_iff`: A prime `p` is primitive in `S` iff `p ‚àà S` and `1 ‚àâ S`.
    41‚Üí-/
    42‚Üí
    43‚Üínamespace Set
    44‚Üí
    45‚Üí/-- An element `n` is *primitive* in a set `S ‚äÜ ‚Ñï` if `n ‚àà S` and no proper divisor of `n`
    46‚Üíis in `S`. -/
    47‚Üídef IsPrimitive (S : Set ‚Ñï) (n : ‚Ñï) : Prop :=
    48‚Üí  n ‚àà S ‚àß Disjoint (n.properDivisors : Set ‚Ñï) S
    49‚Üí
    50‚Üí/-- The set of primitive elements of `S`. -/
    51‚Üídef primitives (S : Set ‚Ñï) : Set ‚Ñï :=
    52‚Üí  {n | S.IsPrimitive n}
    53‚Üí
    54‚Üítheorem IsPrimitive.mem {S : Set ‚Ñï} {n : ‚Ñï} (h : S.IsPrimitive n) : n ‚àà S := h.1
    55‚Üí
    56‚Üítheorem IsPrimitive.disjoint_properDivisors {S : Set ‚Ñï} {n : ‚Ñï} (h : S.IsPrimitive n) :
    57‚Üí    Disjoint (n.properDivisors : Set ‚Ñï) S := h.2
    58‚Üí
    59‚Üítheorem IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_properDivisor {S : Set ‚Ñï} {n d : ‚Ñï} (h : S.IsPrimitive n)
    60‚Üí    (hd : d ‚àà n.properDivisors) : d ‚àâ S :=
    61‚Üí  Set.disjoint_left.mp h.2 hd
    62‚Üí
    63‚Üítheorem IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_dvd_of_lt {S : Set ‚Ñï} {n d : ‚Ñï} (h : S.IsPrimitive n)
    64‚Üí    (hdvd : d ‚à£ n) (hlt : d < n) : d ‚àâ S :=
    65‚Üí  h.not_mem_of_properDivisor (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mpr ‚ü®hdvd, hlt‚ü©)
    66‚Üí
    67‚Üítheorem isPrimitive_iff {S : Set ‚Ñï} {n : ‚Ñï} :
    68‚Üí    S.IsPrimitive n ‚Üî n ‚àà S ‚àß ‚àÄ d ‚àà n.properDivisors, d ‚àâ S := by
    69‚Üí  simp only [IsPrimitive, Set.disjoint_left, Finset.mem_coe]
    70‚Üí
    71‚Üítheorem isPrimitive_iff' {S : Set ‚Ñï} {n : ‚Ñï} (_hn : 0 < n) :
    72‚Üí    S.IsPrimitive n ‚Üî n ‚àà S ‚àß ‚àÄ d, d ‚à£ n ‚Üí d < n ‚Üí d ‚àâ S := by
    73‚Üí  rw [isPrimitive_iff]
    74‚Üí  constructor <;> intro ‚ü®hmem, hdiv‚ü©
    75‚Üí  ¬∑ exact ‚ü®hmem, fun d hd hlt => hdiv d (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mpr ‚ü®hd, hlt‚ü©)‚ü©
    76‚Üí  ¬∑ exact ‚ü®hmem, fun d hd => hdiv d (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mp hd).1 (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mp hd).2‚ü©
    77‚Üí
    78‚Üítheorem mem_primitives_iff {S : Set ‚Ñï} {n : ‚Ñï} : n ‚àà S.primitives ‚Üî S.IsPrimitive n := Iff.rfl
    79‚Üí
    80‚Üí@[simp]
    81‚Üítheorem primitives_subset {S : Set ‚Ñï} : S.primitives ‚äÜ S := fun _ h => h.mem
    82‚Üí
    83‚Üítheorem one_isPrimitive_iff {S : Set ‚Ñï} : S.IsPrimitive 1 ‚Üî 1 ‚àà S := by
    84‚Üí  simp [isPrimitive_iff, Nat.properDivisors_one]
    85‚Üí
    86‚Üítheorem prime_isPrimitive_iff {S : Set ‚Ñï} {p : ‚Ñï} (hp : p.Prime) :
    87‚Üí    S.IsPrimitive p ‚Üî p ‚àà S ‚àß 1 ‚àâ S := by
    88‚Üí  simp [isPrimitive_iff, hp.properDivisors]
    89‚Üí
    90‚Üíend Set
    91‚Üí

    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# Erd≈ës Problem 825
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/825](https://www.erdosproblems.com/825)
    23‚Üí-/
    24‚Üí
    25‚Üíopen scoped ArithmeticFunction.sigma
    26‚Üí
    27‚Üínamespace Erdos825
    28‚Üí
    29‚Üí/--
    30‚ÜíIs there an absolute constant $C > 0$ such that every integer $n$ with
    31‚Üí$\sigma(n) > Cn$ is the distinct sum of proper divisors of $n$?
    32‚Üí
    33‚ÜíThis has been solved in the affirmative by Larsen - in fact, for any $\epsilon>0$ there exists $L$
    34‚Üísuch that if $n$ has only prime divisors $>L$ and $\sigma(n)>(2+\epsilon)n$ then $n$ is the distinct
    35‚Üísum of proper divisors of $n$.
    36‚Üí-/
    37‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    38‚Üítheorem erdos_825 :
    39‚Üí    answer(True) ‚Üî ‚àÉ (C : ‚Ñù) (_ : C > 0),
    40‚Üí      ‚àÄ (n) (_ : œÉ 1 n > C * n),
    41‚Üí        ‚àÉ s ‚äÜ n.properDivisors, n = s.sum id := by
    42‚Üí  sorry
    43‚Üí
    44‚Üí/--
    45‚ÜíShow that if the constant $C > 0$ is such that every integer $n$ with
    46‚Üí$\sigma(n) > Cn$ is the distinct sum of proper divisors of $n$, then we
    47‚Üímust have $C > 2$.
    48‚Üí-/
    49‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    50‚Üítheorem erdos_825.variants.necessary_cond (C : ‚Ñù) (hC : 0 < C)
    51‚Üí    (h : ‚àÄ (n : ‚Ñï) (_ : œÉ 1 n > C * n),
    52‚Üí        ‚àÉ s ‚äÜ n.properDivisors, n = s.sum id) :
    53‚Üí    2 < C := by
    54‚Üí  sorry
    55‚Üí
    56‚Üíend Erdos825
    57‚Üí

    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# Erd≈ës Problem 633
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/633](https://www.erdosproblems.com/633)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíClassify those triangles which can only be cut into a square number of congruent
    25‚Üítriangles.
    26‚Üí
    27‚ÜíAny triangle can be cut into $n^2$ congruent triangles for any $n \geq 1$. Soifer [So09]
    28‚Üíproved that there exists at least one triangle (e.g. one with sides $\sqrt{2}$, $\sqrt{3}$,
    29‚Üí$\sqrt{4}$) which can only be cut into a square number of congruent triangles. In fact,
    30‚ÜíSoifer proved that any triangle for which the angles and sides are both
    31‚Üíintegrally independent has this property. The full classification remains open.
    32‚Üí
    33‚Üí[So09] Soifer, A., *The Mathematical Coloring Book*, Springer, 2009.
    34‚Üí-/
    35‚Üí
    36‚Üínamespace Erdos633
    37‚Üí
    38‚Üí/-- Squared Euclidean distance between two points in $\mathbb{R}^2$. -/
    39‚Üínoncomputable def sqDist (p q : ‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) : ‚Ñù :=
    40‚Üí  (p.1 - q.1) ^ 2 + (p.2 - q.2) ^ 2
    41‚Üí
    42‚Üí/-- Multiset of squared side lengths of a triangle.
    43‚ÜíTwo triangles are congruent (by SSS) iff these multisets agree. -/
    44‚Üínoncomputable def sideLengthsSq (A B C : ‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) : Multiset ‚Ñù :=
    45‚Üí  ‚Üë[sqDist A B, sqDist B C, sqDist A C]
    46‚Üí
    47‚Üí/-- A triangle is non-degenerate (vertices not collinear). -/
    48‚Üídef NonDegenerate (A B C : ‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) : Prop :=
    49‚Üí  (B.1 - A.1) * (C.2 - A.2) - (C.1 - A.1) * (B.2 - A.2) ‚âÝ 0
    50‚Üí
    51‚Üí/-- The closed triangular region: convex hull of vertices $A$, $B$, $C$.
    52‚ÜíA point $p$ lies in the triangle iff $p = \alpha A + \beta B + \gamma C$ for some
    53‚Üí$\alpha, \beta, \gamma \geq 0$ with $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$. -/
    54‚Üídef triangleRegion (A B C : ‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) : Set (‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) :=
    55‚Üí  {p | ‚àÉ (Œ± Œ≤ Œ≥ : ‚Ñù), 0 ‚â§ Œ± ‚àß 0 ‚â§ Œ≤ ‚àß 0 ‚â§ Œ≥ ‚àß Œ± + Œ≤ + Œ≥ = 1 ‚àß
    56‚Üí    p.1 = Œ± * A.1 + Œ≤ * B.1 + Œ≥ * C.1 ‚àß
    57‚Üí    p.2 = Œ± * A.2 + Œ≤ * B.2 + Œ≥ * C.2}
    58‚Üí
    59‚Üí/-- The open interior of a triangular region: strictly positive
    60‚Üíbarycentric coordinates. -/
    61‚Üídef triangleInterior (A B C : ‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) : Set (‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) :=
    62‚Üí  {p | ‚àÉ (Œ± Œ≤ Œ≥ : ‚Ñù), 0 < Œ± ‚àß 0 < Œ≤ ‚àß 0 < Œ≥ ‚àß Œ± + Œ≤ + Œ≥ = 1 ‚àß
    63‚Üí    p.1 = Œ± * A.1 + Œ≤ * B.1 + Œ≥ * C.1 ‚àß
    64‚Üí    p.2 = Œ± * A.2 + Œ≤ * B.2 + Œ≥ * C.2}
    65‚Üí
    66‚Üí/-- Triangle $(A, B, C)$ can be dissected into exactly $n$ pairwise-congruent
    67‚Üínon-degenerate triangles that tile it: each is contained in the original,
    68‚Üítheir interiors are pairwise disjoint, and their union covers the original. -/
    69‚Üídef CanDissectInto (A B C : ‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) (n : ‚Ñï) : Prop :=
    70‚Üí  ‚àÉ (V : Fin n ‚Üí (‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) √ó (‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) √ó (‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù)),
    71‚Üí    -- All sub-triangles are non-degenerate
    72‚Üí    (‚àÄ i, NonDegenerate (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2) ‚àß
    73‚Üí    -- All sub-triangles are mutually congruent (same squared side lengths)
    74‚Üí    (‚àÄ i j, sideLengthsSq (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2 =
    75‚Üí            sideLengthsSq (V j).1 (V j).2.1 (V j).2.2) ‚àß
    76‚Üí    -- Each sub-triangle is contained in the original
    77‚Üí    (‚àÄ i, triangleRegion (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2 ‚äÜ
    78‚Üí           triangleRegion A B C) ‚àß
    79‚Üí    -- Pairwise disjoint interiors
    80‚Üí    (‚àÄ i j, i ‚âÝ j ‚Üí
    81‚Üí      triangleInterior (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2 ‚à©
    82‚Üí      triangleInterior (V j).1 (V j).2.1 (V j).2.2 = ‚àÖ) ‚àß
    83‚Üí    -- Union covers the original triangle
    84‚Üí    (‚àÄ p, p ‚àà triangleRegion A B C ‚Üí
    85‚Üí      ‚àÉ i, p ‚àà triangleRegion (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2)
    86‚Üí
    87‚Üí/-- Euclidean distance (side length) between two points. -/
    88‚Üínoncomputable def sideLength (p q : ‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) : ‚Ñù :=
    89‚Üí  Real.sqrt (sqDist p q)
    90‚Üí
    91‚Üí/-- The sides of a triangle are integrally independent: no nontrivial integer
    92‚Üílinear combination of the three side lengths equals zero. -/
    93‚Üídef IntIndepSides (A B C : ‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) : Prop :=
    94‚Üí  ‚àÄ (a‚ÇÅ a‚ÇÇ a‚ÇÉ : ‚Ñ§),
    95‚Üí    ‚Üëa‚ÇÅ * sideLength B C + ‚Üëa‚ÇÇ * sideLength A C +
    96‚Üí      ‚Üëa‚ÇÉ * sideLength A B = 0 ‚Üí
    97‚Üí    a‚ÇÅ = 0 ‚àß a‚ÇÇ = 0 ‚àß a‚ÇÉ = 0
    98‚Üí
    99‚Üí/-- The angle at vertex $P$ in triangle $PQR$, via the dot-product formula:
   100‚Üí$\arccos\bigl(\frac{\vec{PQ} \cdot \vec{PR}}{|PQ| \cdot |PR|}\bigr)$.
   101‚ÜíReturns a value in $[0, \pi]$. -/
   102‚Üínoncomputable def angle (P Q R : ‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) : ‚Ñù :=
   103‚Üí  Real.arccos (((Q.1 - P.1) * (R.1 - P.1) + (Q.2 - P.2) * (R.2 - P.2)) /
   104‚Üí    (sideLength P Q * sideLength P R))
   105‚Üí
   106‚Üí/-- The angles of a triangle are integrally independent: the only integer
   107‚Üílinear combination of the angles that equals an integer multiple of $\pi$
   108‚Üíis the trivial one (all coefficients equal). This accounts for the
   109‚Üíconstraint $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = \pi$. -/
   110‚Üídef IntIndepAngles (A B C : ‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) : Prop :=
   111‚Üí  ‚àÄ (a‚ÇÅ a‚ÇÇ a‚ÇÉ k : ‚Ñ§),
   112‚Üí    ‚Üëa‚ÇÅ * angle A B C + ‚Üëa‚ÇÇ * angle B A C +
   113‚Üí      ‚Üëa‚ÇÉ * angle C A B = ‚Üëk * Real.pi ‚Üí
   114‚Üí    a‚ÇÅ = a‚ÇÇ ‚àß a‚ÇÇ = a‚ÇÉ
   115‚Üí
   116‚Üí/--
   117‚ÜíEvery non-degenerate triangle can be dissected into $n^2$ congruent triangles
   118‚Üífor any positive integer $n$.
   119‚Üí-/
   120‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 51]
   121‚Üítheorem erdos_633.variants.square_dissection
   122‚Üí    (A B C : ‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) (hnd : NonDegenerate A B C)
   123‚Üí    (n : ‚Ñï) (hn : 1 ‚â§ n) :
   124‚Üí    CanDissectInto A B C (n ^ 2) := by
   125‚Üí  sorry
   126‚Üí
   127‚Üí/--
   128‚ÜíThere exists a non-degenerate triangle which can be dissected into $n$
   129‚Üícongruent triangles only when $n$ is a perfect square. [So09]
   130‚Üí-/
   131‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 51]
   132‚Üítheorem erdos_633.variants.existence :
   133‚Üí    ‚àÉ A B C : ‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù, NonDegenerate A B C ‚àß
   134‚Üí    ‚àÄ n : ‚Ñï, 1 ‚â§ n ‚Üí CanDissectInto A B C n ‚Üí ‚àÉ m : ‚Ñï, n = m ^ 2 := by
   135‚Üí  sorry
   136‚Üí
   137‚Üí/--
   138‚ÜíIf a non-degenerate triangle has integrally independent sides and angles,
   139‚Üíthen it can only be dissected into a perfect square number of congruent
   140‚Üítriangles. [So09]
   141‚Üí-/
   142‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 51]
   143‚Üítheorem erdos_633.variants.soifer
   144‚Üí    (A B C : ‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) (hnd : NonDegenerate A B C)
   145‚Üí    (hsides : IntIndepSides A B C) (hangles : IntIndepAngles A B C) :
   146‚Üí    ‚àÄ n : ‚Ñï, 1 ‚â§ n ‚Üí CanDissectInto A B C n ‚Üí ‚àÉ m : ‚Ñï, n = m ^ 2 := by
   147‚Üí  sorry
   148‚Üí
   149‚Üí/--
   150‚ÜíClassify those non-degenerate triangles that can only be cut into a perfect
   151‚Üísquare number of congruent triangles. The full classification remains open.
   152‚Üí-/
   153‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 51]
   154‚Üítheorem erdos_633 :
   155‚Üí    {T : (‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) √ó (‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) √ó (‚Ñù √ó ‚Ñù) |
   156‚Üí      NonDegenerate T.1 T.2.1 T.2.2 ‚àß
   157‚Üí      ‚àÄ n, 1 ‚â§ n ‚Üí CanDissectInto T.1 T.2.1 T.2.2 n ‚Üí ‚àÉ m, n = m ^ 2} =
   158‚Üí    answer(sorry) := by sorry
   159‚Üí
   160‚Üíend Erdos633
   161‚Üí

    18‚Üí
    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# Erd≈ës Problem 630
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/630](https://www.erdosproblems.com/630)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíErd≈ës, Rubin, and Taylor asked whether every planar bipartite graph is 3-choosable
    25‚Üí(i.e., 3-list-colorable). This was proved by Alon and Tarsi.
    26‚Üí
    27‚Üí[ERT80] Erd≈ës, P., Rubin, A. L., and Taylor, H., _Choosability in graphs_. Proceedings of the
    28‚ÜíWest Coast Conference on Combinatorics, Graph Theory and Computing (1980).
    29‚Üí
    30‚Üí[AlTa92] Alon, N. and Tarsi, M., _Colorings and orientations of graphs_. Combinatorica 12
    31‚Üí(1992), 125-134.
    32‚Üí-/
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üíopen SimpleGraph
    35‚Üí
    36‚Üínamespace Erdos630
    37‚Üí
    38‚Üí/-- A proper list coloring of $G$ with respect to a list assignment $L : V \to \text{Finset}\ \mathbb{N}$
    39‚Üíis a function $f : V \to \mathbb{N}$ such that $f(v) \in L(v)$ for all $v$, and $f(u) \neq f(v)$
    40‚Üíwhenever $u$ and $v$ are adjacent. -/
    41‚Üídef IsProperListColoring {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) (L : V ‚Üí Finset ‚Ñï)
    42‚Üí    (f : V ‚Üí ‚Ñï) : Prop :=
    43‚Üí  (‚àÄ v, f v ‚àà L v) ‚àß (‚àÄ u v, G.Adj u v ‚Üí f u ‚âÝ f v)
    44‚Üí
    45‚Üí/-- A graph $G$ is $k$-choosable ($k$-list-colorable) if for every list assignment $L$
    46‚Üíwhere each vertex receives a list of at least $k$ colors, there exists a
    47‚Üíproper list coloring. -/
    48‚Üídef IsChoosable {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) (k : ‚Ñï) : Prop :=
    49‚Üí  ‚àÄ L : V ‚Üí Finset ‚Ñï, (‚àÄ v, k ‚â§ (L v).card) ‚Üí
    50‚Üí    ‚àÉ f : V ‚Üí ‚Ñï, IsProperListColoring G L f
    51‚Üí
    52‚Üí/-- The list chromatic number (choice number) of a graph $G$: the minimum $k$
    53‚Üísuch that $G$ is $k$-choosable. -/
    54‚Üínoncomputable def listChromaticNumber {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : ‚Ñï :=
    55‚Üí  sInf {k : ‚Ñï | IsChoosable G k}
    56‚Üí
    57‚Üí/-- A graph is planar if it can be embedded in the plane without edge crossings.
    58‚ÜíMathlib does not yet have a formalization of graph planarity; we axiomatize it
    59‚Üíhere as an opaque predicate. -/
    60‚Üíopaque IsPlanar {V : Type*} [Fintype V] (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop
    61‚Üí
    62‚Üí/--
    63‚ÜíErd≈ës Problem 630 [ERT80]:
    64‚Üí
    65‚ÜíEvery planar bipartite graph $G$ is $3$-choosable, i.e., $\chi_L(G) \leq 3$.
    66‚Üí
    67‚ÜíProved by Alon and Tarsi [AlTa92].
    68‚Üí-/
    69‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    70‚Üítheorem erdos_630 {V : Type*} [Fintype V] (G : SimpleGraph V)
    71‚Üí    (hplanar : IsPlanar G) (hbip : G.Colorable 2) :
    72‚Üí    IsChoosable G 3 := by
    73‚Üí  sorry
    74‚Üí
    75‚Üíend Erdos630
    76‚Üí

    16‚Üí
    17‚Üíimport FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18‚Üí
    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# Erd≈ës Problem 468
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/468](https://www.erdosproblems.com/468)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíFor any $n$ let $D_n$ be the set of partial sums $d_1, d_1+d_2, d_1+d_2+d_3, \ldots$
    25‚Üíwhere $1 < d_1 < d_2 < \cdots$ are the divisors of $n$.
    26‚Üí
    27‚ÜíIf $f(N)$ is the minimal $n$ such that $N \in D_n$, is it true that $f(N) = o(N)$?
    28‚ÜíPerhaps just for almost all $N$?
    29‚Üí
    30‚Üí[ErGr80] Erd≈ës, P. and Graham, R., _Old and new problems and results in combinatorial
    31‚Üínumber theory_. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
    32‚Üí-/
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üíopen Finset Filter
    35‚Üí
    36‚Üínamespace Erdos468
    37‚Üí
    38‚Üí/-- Prefix sums of a list with accumulator.
    39‚ÜíReturns $[\mathrm{acc}+a_1, \mathrm{acc}+a_1+a_2, \ldots]$. -/
    40‚Üídef prefixSums : List ‚Ñï ‚Üí ‚Ñï ‚Üí List ‚Ñï
    41‚Üí  | [], _ => []
    42‚Üí  | a :: as, acc => (acc + a) :: prefixSums as (acc + a)
    43‚Üí
    44‚Üí/-- $D_n$: the set of partial sums of the divisors of $n$ that are greater than $1$,
    45‚Üítaken in increasing order. -/
    46‚Üídef D (n : ‚Ñï) : Finset ‚Ñï :=
    47‚Üí  (prefixSums ((n.divisors.filter (1 < ¬∑)).sort (¬∑ ‚â§ ¬∑)) 0).toFinset
    48‚Üí
    49‚Üí/-- $f(N)$: the minimal $n$ such that $N \in D_n$. -/
    50‚Üínoncomputable def f (N : ‚Ñï) : ‚Ñï :=
    51‚Üí  sInf {n : ‚Ñï | N ‚àà D n}
    52‚Üí
    53‚Üí/--
    54‚ÜíErd≈ës Problem 468, strong form [ErGr80]:
    55‚Üí
    56‚ÜíIf $f(N)$ is the minimal $n$ such that $N \in D_n$, then $f(N) = o(N)$.
    57‚Üí-/
    58‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 11]
    59‚Üítheorem erdos_468 : answer(sorry) ‚Üî
    60‚Üí    ‚àÄ Œµ : ‚Ñù, 0 < Œµ ‚Üí
    61‚Üí      ‚àÉ N‚ÇÄ : ‚Ñï, ‚àÄ N : ‚Ñï, N‚ÇÄ ‚â§ N ‚Üí
    62‚Üí        (f N : ‚Ñù) < Œµ * (N : ‚Ñù) := by
    63‚Üí  sorry
    64‚Üí
    65‚Üí/--
    66‚ÜíErd≈ës Problem 468, weak form [ErGr80]:
    67‚Üí
    68‚Üí$f(N) = o(N)$ for almost all $N$. Formalized as: for any $\varepsilon > 0$, the density of
    69‚Üí$\{N \le x : f(N) \ge \varepsilon \cdot N\}$ tends to $0$ as $x \to \infty$.
    70‚Üí-/
    71‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 11]
    72‚Üítheorem erdos_468.variants.weak : answer(sorry) ‚Üî
    73‚Üí    ‚àÄ Œµ : ‚Ñù, 0 < Œµ ‚Üí
    74‚Üí      Tendsto (fun x : ‚Ñï =>
    75‚Üí        (((Finset.Icc 1 x).filter (fun N =>
    76‚Üí          (f N : ‚Ñù) ‚â• Œµ * (N : ‚Ñù))).card : ‚Ñù) / (x : ‚Ñù))
    77‚Üí        atTop (nhds 0) := by
    78‚Üí  sorry
    79‚Üí
    80‚Üíend Erdos468
    81‚Üí

    20‚Üí# Erd≈ës Problem 69
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/69](https://www.erdosproblems.com/69)
    23‚Üí-/

    29‚Üí/--

    32‚Üí\sum_{n\geq 2}\frac{\omega(n)}{2^n}
    33‚Üí$$
    34‚Üíirrational? (Here $\omega(n)$ counts the number of distinct prime divisors of $n$.)
    35‚Üí-/
    36‚Üí@[category undergraduate, AMS 11]
    37‚Üítheorem erdos_69 : Irrational <| ‚àë' n, œâ (n + 2) / 2 ^ (n + 2) := by

    43‚Üí\sum_{n\geq 2}\frac{\omega(n)}{2^n} = \sum_p \frac{1}{2^p - 1}.
    44‚Üí$$

    46‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 11]

    49‚Üí    ‚àë' n, œâ (n + 2) / (2 ^ (n + 2) : ‚Ñù) = ‚àë' p : A, 1 / (2 ^ p.1 - 1) := by

    52‚Üíend Erdos69

    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# Erd≈ës Problem 48
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/48](https://www.erdosproblems.com/48)

    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/958](https://www.erdosproblems.com/958)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíLet $A \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be a finite set of size $n$, and let $\{d_1, \ldots, d_k\}$
    25‚Üíbe the set of distinct distances determined by $A$. Let $f(d)$ be the multiplicity of $d$,
    26‚Üíthe number of unordered pairs from $A$ at distance $d$ apart.
    27‚Üí
    28‚ÜíIs it true that $k = n - 1$ and $\{f(d_i)\} = \{1, \ldots, n-1\}$ if and only if $A$ is
    29‚Üía set of equidistant points on a line or a circle?
    30‚Üí
    31‚ÜíErd≈ës conjectured that the answer is no, and other such configurations exist [Er84c].
    32‚Üí
    33‚ÜíThis was proved by Clemen, Dumitrescu, and Liu [CDL25], who observed that equidistant
    34‚Üípoints on a short circular arc on a circle of radius 1, together with the centre, provide
    35‚Üía counterexample.
    36‚Üí
    37‚Üí[Er84c] Erd≈ës, P., _Some old and new problems on combinatorial geometry_, 1984.
    38‚Üí
    39‚Üí[CDL25] Clemen, F., Dumitrescu, A., and Liu, Y., 2025.
    40‚Üí-/
    41‚Üí
    42‚Üínamespace Erdos958
    43‚Üí
    44‚Üí/-- The set of distinct distances determined by a finite point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$. -/
    45‚Üínoncomputable def distinctDistances (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ‚Ñù (Fin 2))) : Finset ‚Ñù :=
    46‚Üí  ((A √óÀ¢ A).filter (fun p => p.1 ‚âÝ p.2)).image (fun p => dist p.1 p.2)
    47‚Üí
    48‚Üí/-- The number of unordered pairs in $A$ at distance $d$. -/
    49‚Üínoncomputable def distMultiplicity (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ‚Ñù (Fin 2))) (d : ‚Ñù) : ‚Ñï :=
    50‚Üí  ((A √óÀ¢ A).filter (fun p => p.1 ‚âÝ p.2 ‚àß dist p.1 p.2 = d)).card / 2
    51‚Üí
    52‚Üí/-- The distance multiplicity property: $A$ has exactly $n-1$ distinct distances and the
    53‚Üímultiplicities are exactly $\{1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$. -/
    54‚Üídef HasDistanceMultiplicityProperty (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ‚Ñù (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    55‚Üí  A.card ‚â• 2 ‚àß
    56‚Üí  (distinctDistances A).card = A.card - 1 ‚àß
    57‚Üí  ‚àÄ m : ‚Ñï, (1 ‚â§ m ‚àß m ‚â§ A.card - 1) ‚Üî
    58‚Üí    (‚àÉ d ‚àà distinctDistances A, distMultiplicity A d = m)
    59‚Üí
    60‚Üí/-- A finite point set is equidistant-collinear if the points are equally spaced along a
    61‚Üíline: $A = \{p, p + v, p + 2v, \ldots, p + (n-1)v\}$ for some $p, v$ with $v \neq 0$. -/
    62‚Üídef IsEquidistantCollinear (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ‚Ñù (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    63‚Üí  ‚àÉ (p v : EuclideanSpace ‚Ñù (Fin 2)), v ‚âÝ 0 ‚àß
    64‚Üí    ‚àÄ a ‚àà A, ‚àÉ i : Fin A.card, a = p + (i.val : ‚Ñù) ‚Ä¢ v
    65‚Üí
    66‚Üí/-- A finite point set is equidistant-circular if the points are equally spaced on a
    67‚Üícircle: all points lie on a circle and consecutive points in a cyclic ordering have
    68‚Üíequal Euclidean distance. -/
    69‚Üídef IsEquidistantCircular (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ‚Ñù (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    70‚Üí  A.card ‚â• 3 ‚àß
    71‚Üí  ‚àÉ (c : EuclideanSpace ‚Ñù (Fin 2)) (r : ‚Ñù), r > 0 ‚àß
    72‚Üí    (‚àÄ a ‚àà A, dist a c = r) ‚àß
    73‚Üí    ‚àÉ (œÉ : ‚Ñï ‚Üí EuclideanSpace ‚Ñù (Fin 2)),
    74‚Üí      (‚àÄ i, i < A.card ‚Üí œÉ i ‚àà A) ‚àß
    75‚Üí      (‚àÄ a ‚àà A, ‚àÉ i, i < A.card ‚àß œÉ i = a) ‚àß
    76‚Üí      (‚àÄ i j, i < A.card ‚Üí j < A.card ‚Üí œÉ i = œÉ j ‚Üí i = j) ‚àß
    77‚Üí      ‚àÄ i, i < A.card ‚Üí
    78‚Üí        dist (œÉ i) (œÉ ((i + 1) % A.card)) = dist (œÉ 0) (œÉ 1)
    79‚Üí
    80‚Üí/--
    81‚Üí**Erd≈ës Problem 958** [Er84c]:
    82‚Üí
    83‚ÜíIs it true that if a finite set $A \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ satisfies the distance multiplicity
    84‚Üíproperty (exactly $n-1$ distinct distances with multiplicities $\{1, \ldots, n-1\}$), then $A$
    85‚Üímust be a set of equidistant points on a line or equidistant points on a circle?
    86‚Üí
    87‚ÜíThe answer is no. This was proved by Clemen, Dumitrescu, and Liu [CDL25].
    88‚Üí-/
    89‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    90‚Üítheorem erdos_958 : answer(False) ‚Üî
    91‚Üí    (‚àÄ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ‚Ñù (Fin 2)),
    92‚Üí      HasDistanceMultiplicityProperty A ‚Üí
    93‚Üí      (IsEquidistantCollinear A ‚à® IsEquidistantCircular A)) := by
    94‚Üí  sorry
    95‚Üí
    96‚Üíend Erdos958

    24‚ÜíLet $h(x)$ count the number of integers $1 \le a < b < x$ such that $(a,b) = 1$ and
    25‚Üí$\sigma(a) = \sigma(b)$, where $\sigma$ is the sum of divisors function.

    31‚ÜíPomerance [PoPo16].

    50‚Üíwith equal sum-of-divisors values. -/

    53‚Üí    (fun p => 0 < p.1 ‚àß p.1 < p.2 ‚àß Nat.Coprime p.1 p.2 ‚àß
    54‚Üí      sumDivisors p.1 = sumDivisors p.2)).card
